content.assessment: From a network-planning perspective, this proposal redesigns admission mechanics without altering the geographic footprint of the 6/8-year network. Since selective schools remain concentrated in urban settlements and Budapest (F-live-school_network_planning-05), and the secondary network's proximity guarantee applies to generic secondary schools rather than selective clustered tracks (F-live-school_network_planning-06), an SES-weighted exam redesign does not by itself address distance-to-school access gaps. Capacity itself is untouched: the stagnant ~25,962 places (F-live-school_network_planning-03) remain the ceiling regardless of how the exam sorts within that ceiling.
opportunities: Could be paired with geographic-access data to identify where SES-weighting would need supplementary transport or dormitory provision
strengths: Works within the existing centrally-administered exam infrastructure rather than requiring new school-network capacity or facilities
Does not require reallocation of physical places, avoiding transport or capacity disruption
threats: Distance/access non-randomness (implied by geographic clustering, F-live-school_network_planning-05) could mean SES-index benefits are captured disproportionately by urban disadvantaged families while rural disadvantaged families still cannot reach a network node
weaknesses: Does not address the geographic concentration of six/eight-year gimnázium capacity in urban/Budapest areas (F-live-school_network_planning-05)
Leaves the stagnant capacity ceiling of ~25,962 places unchanged, so oversubscription pressure at flagship schools persists (F-live-school_network_planning-03)
Feeder-school dilution dynamics (F-live-school_network_planning-07) are untouched since demand concentration on selective urban schools continues regardless of exam format
